speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Your hemoglobin level today is 111 g/L — slightly below the normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: I see your body temperature is elevated at 39.3 °C — you're running a fever. Have you had chills or any recent infections?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I've felt quite unwell since yesterday — fatigue, some shortness of breath, and my ankles have been swelling.

speaker: doctor
text: You're currently on Insulin glargine 10 units nightly for diabetes management. Are you taking it consistently?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, every night before bed — though I did skip it two nights ago because I wasn’t eating much.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history and today’s findings, your primary diagnosis remains Coronary Artery Disease. We’ll need to monitor cardiac strain closely given your symptoms.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 132 mmHg — within acceptable limits for now, though we’ll watch it carefully.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed my legs feel heavy, and there’s noticeable puffiness around my ankles and feet.

speaker: patient
text: Yes — lower limb edema has been getting worse over the past three days, especially by evening.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose this morning was 13.3 mmol/L — significantly elevated, which may be contributing to your symptoms.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your hypertension, CAD, and worsening edema, I’m going to switch you from your previous ACE inhibitor to Lisinopril 20 mg once daily.

speaker: doctor
text: Interestingly, your repeat hemoglobin came back at 87 g/L — that’s a notable drop from the 111 g/L we saw earlier today. This suggests possible acute blood loss or hemolysis we need to investigate urgently.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure has risen to 149 mmHg — higher than the 132 mmHg we recorded earlier. That, combined with the edema and fever, raises concern for decompensated heart failure.

speaker: doctor
text: Your latest blood glucose is now 16.0 mmol/L — up from 13.3 mmol/L earlier. That degree of hyperglycemia could be worsening your fluid retention and cardiac workload.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been drinking more water but still feel very thirsty and urinating frequently.

speaker: doctor
text: Those are classic signs of uncontrolled diabetes. We’ll adjust your insulin regimen and add close glucose monitoring. Do you have a home glucometer?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I check twice a day — fasting and before dinner. But lately the numbers have been all over the place.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll arrange urgent labs including CBC, renal panel, BNP, and HbA1c — and possibly an echocardiogram given your edema, elevated BP, and CAD history.

speaker: patient
text: Should I keep taking the insulin as prescribed until I see you again?

speaker: patient
text: Actually, I just checked at home — that reading matches the 13.3 mmol/L you noted earlier, and my cuff gave me 132 mmHg again, same as your first measurement.
